unit: Unit 8 Leak Detection, System Evacuation, and System Cleanup
section: Section 2 Safety Tools and Equipment and Shop Practices

slides.title: Leaks and Leak Checking
bullets: All sealed systems leak
Flaws exist at every joint fitting seam or weld
Flaws may be too small to detect but can grow with time vibration temperature and environmental stress

slides.title: Exposing the Leak Site
bullets: Vapor can flow under layers of paint flux rust slag and pipe insulation
Important to clean the leak site by removing loose paint slag flux or rust
Remove pipe insulation oil and grease to avoid contaminating detection tips

slides.title: Standing Pressure and Temperature Leaks
bullets: Standing leaks detected while the unit is at rest or off (most common)
Pressure-dependent leaks detected as system pressure increases
Temperature-dependent leaks associated with heat of expansion

slides.title: Vibration Combination and Cumulative Leaks
bullets: Vibration-dependent leaks occur during unit operation
Combination-dependent leaks require two or more conditions to leak
Cumulative microleaks are many individual leaks too small to detect with standard tools

slides.title: Basic Refrigerant Leak Detection
bullets: Spot refrigerant oil residue for standing leaks
Test evaporator section leaks
Test condensing section leaks
Test suction- and liquid-line leaks

slides.title: Advanced Leak Detection
bullets: Testing for pressure-dependent leaks
Testing for temperature-dependent leaks
Testing for vibration-dependent leaks
Testing for combination-dependent leaks

slides.title: Standing Pressure Test
bullets: Visually inspect newly assembled system
Solder joints should have no gaps
Flanged and threaded connections should be tight
Control valves should be installed properly
All service valve covers should be in place

slides.title: Visual of a Standing Pressure Test
bullets: Figure 8.12 Isolating the compressor to pressurize the condenser and evaporator

slides.title: Performing a Standing Pressure Test
bullets: Pressurize system with dry nitrogen to no higher than lowest system test pressure
Allow system to rest for ten minutes
Mark needle positions on gauge manifold
Monitor gauge needle position

slides.title: Leak Detection Methods
bullets: Audible leaks are large and relatively easy to locate
Halide leak detector
Ultrasonic leak detector
Ultraviolet leak detectors
Electronic leak detectors

slides.title: Ultrasonic Leak Detector
bullets: Figure 8.14 An ultrasonic leak detector that listens for leaks

slides.title: Using an Ultraviolet Leak Detector
bullets: Inject oil-specific dye into system through manifold gauge set
Use a black light to locate refrigerant leak

slides.title: Leak Detection Tips
bullets: System must be evacuated to required vacuum levels
Visually inspect for oil and dirt spots
Leaks can be caused by vibration or temperature
Leak check gauge ports prior to gauge installation
Start at the highest point and work downward
Do not rush

slides.title: Repairing Leaks
bullets: Usually not as economical as adding refrigerant
Best field practice is to repair system leaks whenever possible
EPA establishes requirements for when leaks must be repaired
Repair requirements may depend on system type refrigerant used and total system charge

slides.title: Purpose of System Evacuation
bullets: Air contains oxygen nitrogen and water vapor
Nitrogen is a non-condensable gas
Non-condensables cause a rise in system operating head pressure
Oxygen and water vapor cause chemical reactions producing acids electroplating and motor insulation breakdown

slides.title: Degassing and Dehydration
bullets: Chemical combinations create hydrofluoric or hydrochloric acids
Evacuation equals degassing plus dehydration
Moisture plus acid plus oil equals sludge
Sludge can plug system components
Proper evacuation can eliminate acid and sludge formation

slides.title: Evacuation Theory
bullets: Pulling a vacuum lowers system pressure below atmospheric pressure (14.696 psia or 29.93 in. Hg)
Degassing removes non-condensable gases
Systems should be evacuated from high and low pressure sides

slides.title: Measuring the Vacuum
bullets: Pressures below 1 mm Hg are measured in microns
1000 microns = 1 mm; 1 in. = 25,400 microns
Micron gauge displays can be electronic analog digital or LED
Gauge should be located as far as possible from the vacuum pump
Valve off the vacuum pump when desired vacuum is reached and mark the reading

slides.title: Electronic Micron and Vacuum Gauges
bullets: Figure 8.22 Modern handheld digital electronic micron gauges

slides.title: Recovering Refrigerant
bullets: If refrigerant is or has been in the system it must be removed before evacuating
Use EPA-approved recovery equipment

slides.title: The Vacuum Pump
bullets: Two-stage rotary vacuum pumps produce the lowest vacuum
Usual vacuum required by manufacturers is approximately 500 microns
Very low vacuums cause moisture to boil to vapor which is removed by the pump

slides.title: Deep Vacuum
bullets: Measured in the 250- to 500-micron range
Once proper micron levels are reached the vacuum pump is valved off
If pressure remains constant no noncondensable gas or moisture is left in the system

slides.title: Multiple or Triple Evacuation
bullets: Evacuate to about 1,500 microns
Allow a small amount of dry nitrogen to bleed into the system
Evacuate again to 1,500 microns
Repeat three times until last vacuum reaches 250 to 500 microns

slides.title: Leak Detection (Vacuum Gauge Method)
bullets: Watching for pressure rise on a vacuum gauge is not recommended
If there is a leak air can enter the system during the test
This method only proves system will not leak under a pressure difference of atmospheric pressure

slides.title: Removing Moisture with a Vacuum
bullets: Apply heat to system when moisture is present to vaporize water
Vacuum pump will remove the vapor

slides.title: General Evacuation Procedures
bullets: Use a cold trap when appropriate
Do not start a hermetic compressor while in a deep vacuum
Apply heat to compressor to assist in removing water under the oil
Gauge manifolds with large valve ports and hoses help speed evacuation
Schrader stem depressors can be removed

slides.title: Systems with Schrader Valves
bullets: Systems with Schrader valves take longer to evacuate than systems with service valves
Field service valves replace Schrader stems while system is under pressure
Replace Schrader valve caps after service

slides.title: Gauge Manifold Hoses and System Valves
bullets: Hoses can have pinhole leaks slowing evacuation
Using copper tubing for gauge lines helps eliminate evacuation problems
Check system valves to verify they are open before evacuation
Closed valves can trap air in the system

slides.title: Using Dry Nitrogen and Evacuation Without a Manifold
bullets: Sweep dry nitrogen through a sealed system to keep atmosphere out and clean piping
Evacuating without a manifold reduces resistance to flow shortens evacuation time and produces deeper vacuum levels

slides.title: Cleaning a Dirty System
bullets: Heat can cause refrigerant and oil to break down forming acid soot and sludge
Sludge cannot be removed with a vacuum pump
Fumes can be toxic
Wear safety goggles and gloves
